findings.notes: Dependency-free CommonJS function converting a string/number to a lowercase, hyphen-collapsed, edge-trimmed, length-capped URL slug; importable as-is.
Single default CommonJS export; consumers integrate via const slugify = require('./slugify') — the sole connection surface.
Zero external dependencies, no I/O, no global state — safe drop-in with no transitive install cost.
ES5 idioms: 'use strict' (line 1), var declarations, and the maxLen = maxLen || 60 default rather than ES6 default parameters — match this style when extending.
Purpose is documented in a JSDoc block (lines 3-7) above the function; follow this doc style for any added behavior.
Edge-hyphen strip (line 21) runs BEFORE the length cap (lines 24-26), so slice(0,maxLen) can reintroduce a trailing hyphen — violates spec requirements 4 and 5 that truncation keep the slug valid.

Only numbers are stringified (line 13); null/undefined/boolean/object fall through and throw TypeError at toLowerCase (line 15), yet tasks.md claims 'Input validation complete' — a spec-vs-code contradiction.
maxLen = maxLen || 60 treats a falsy 0 as unset, so a caller cannot request a zero-length cap; a negative maxLen also mis-slices via String.slice.
Inputs like '' or '!!!' return an empty string with no fallback or error; spec does not define behavior for an empty resulting slug.

Non-ASCII/accented characters are dropped as separators rather than transliterated (é becomes a hyphen, so 'Café'->'caf'); spec's caf-menu example accepts this, but it is a reuse limitation for non-ASCII titles.
